title: Elements, compounds and mixtures — the building blocks of chemistry
examTip: Mixtures are separated by physical methods; compounds can only be separated by chemical reactions. Examiners love asking you to pick the right separation technique and justify it.
introTips: Everything around you — air, water, your phone, your food — is made of elements, compounds or mixtures.
Knowing which category a substance falls into tells you exactly how to separate it and what properties to expect.
This topic underpins the whole of chemistry — get it solid now and the rest of the course becomes much easier.
intro: Why does salt dissolve in water but sand doesn't? Why can you get pure water from salt water just by boiling it, but you can't "unburn" magnesium back into its starting materials? The answer lies in understanding the difference between elements, compounds and mixtures. This isn't just classification for the sake of it — it tells you exactly what a substance is made of, how it behaves, and crucially, how to separate it. Every practical technique you'll ever do in a chemistry lab is built on these three ideas.

keyPoints.heading: Elements
keyPoints.content: An element is a pure substance made of only one type of atom. It cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical means — it's the most fundamental level of chemistry. There are 118 known elements, each with a unique symbol. The symbol is always 1 or 2 letters, with the first letter always a capital: Na (sodium), Fe (iron), Cl (chlorine), O (oxygen), C (carbon), Au (gold). Some symbols come from Latin names — Fe from ferrum, Au from aurum. Elements are organised in the periodic table: metals are on the left and centre, non-metals on the right.
writeBullets: Element — a pure substance containing only one type of atom
Cannot be broken down by chemical means — it's already at the simplest level
118 known elements, each with a unique 1- or 2-letter symbol
First letter of symbol is always a capital (e.g. Na, Fe, Cl, O, C, Au)
Metals on left/centre of periodic table; non-metals on the right
Some symbols come from Latin names (Fe = ferrum, Au = aurum)

keyTerms.def: A pure substance made of only one type of atom; cannot be broken down by chemical means
A 1- or 2-letter code representing an element, with the first letter always capitalised

keyPoints.memTip: If a substance is just ONE type of atom repeated — it's an element. Think of iron (all Fe atoms) or oxygen gas (all O atoms).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse elements with pure substances. Reinforce: all elements are pure, but not all pure substances are elements (a compound can also be pure).

keyPoints.heading: Compounds
keyPoints.content: A compound is formed when two or more elements are chemically bonded together. This is a key point: the atoms aren't just mixed — they're joined by chemical bonds, which means energy is needed to separate them again. Compounds have completely different properties from the elements they're made from. For example: sodium is a reactive metal that explodes in water; chlorine is a toxic yellow-green gas. But sodium chloride (NaCl) — table salt — is perfectly safe to eat. Compounds always have a fixed ratio of atoms — water is always H₂O (2 hydrogen : 1 oxygen), never H₃O or HO. The name of a compound often gives clues about its composition: names ending in -ide usually contain two elements only (e.g. sodium chloride, magnesium oxide); names ending in -ate or -ite usually contain oxygen as well (e.g. calcium carbonate, copper sulfate).
writeBullets: Compound — two or more elements chemically bonded together in a fixed ratio
Has different properties from the elements it's made from (e.g. Na + Cl → NaCl)
Fixed ratio of atoms — water is always H₂O, never HO or H₃O
Can only be separated by chemical reactions (not physical methods)
Name ending -ide = two elements only (sodium chloride, magnesium oxide)
Name ending -ate or -ite = contains oxygen too (calcium carbonate, copper sulfate)

keyTerms.def: A substance formed when two or more elements are chemically bonded together in a fixed ratio
Suffix indicating a compound made of only two elements (e.g. sodium chloride, magnesium oxide)
Suffix indicating a compound that contains oxygen alongside other elements (e.g. calcium carbonate)

keyPoints.memTip: COMPOUND = atoms CHEMically bonded. You need CHEMistry to break them apart. If you can separate it by heating or filtering — it's probably a mixture.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The sodium + chlorine → sodium chloride example is brilliant for showing how compounds differ from their constituent elements. Use it every time.

keyPoints.heading: Mixtures
keyPoints.content: A mixture contains two or more substances that are not chemically combined — each substance keeps its own properties. The composition can vary (unlike a compound with its fixed ratio). Mixtures can be separated by physical methods because no chemical bonds need to be broken. Common examples: air (nitrogen + oxygen + argon + CO₂ + others), salt water (NaCl dissolved in H₂O), crude oil (many different hydrocarbons), brass (copper + zinc). The right separation technique depends on the properties of the substances in the mixture. Key techniques: filtration separates an insoluble solid from a liquid; evaporation removes the solvent to leave a dissolved solid; crystallisation produces pure crystals by controlled cooling; distillation separates liquids by boiling point; chromatography separates dissolved substances by how far they travel in a solvent.
writeBullets: Mixture — two or more substances not chemically combined; each keeps its own properties
Variable composition — any ratio of components is possible
Separated by physical methods (no chemical bonds to break)
Filtration separates insoluble solids from liquids
Distillation separates liquids with different boiling points
Chromatography separates dissolved substances by solubility and attraction to paper

keyTerms.def: Two or more substances not chemically combined; variable composition; separated by physical means
Separates an insoluble solid from a liquid using a filter paper
Separates liquids with different boiling points by evaporation and condensation
Separates dissolved substances based on their different solubilities and attractions to the stationary phase

keyPoints.memTip: MIXTURE = substances just MIXED together. Think of a fruit salad — the apple is still apple, the banana is still banana. They haven't become 'applebanana'.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say distillation 'purifies' water — remind them it separates, but the condensed water is pure only if you're separating it from a dissolved solid.

keyPoints.heading: Separation techniques in detail
keyPoints.content: Filtration: the mixture is poured through filter paper in a funnel. The insoluble solid (residue) is trapped by the filter paper; the liquid (filtrate) passes through. Used to separate sand from water. Evaporation: the solution is heated so the solvent evaporates, leaving the dissolved solid behind. Quick but can leave impurities and doesn't give large crystals. Crystallisation: the solution is heated to concentrate it, then allowed to cool slowly — crystals form as solubility decreases with temperature. Produces purer, larger crystals than evaporation. Simple distillation: solution heated → solvent evaporates → vapour travels through condenser → condenses back to liquid in collecting flask. Separates a solvent from a dissolved solid (e.g. pure water from salt water). Fractional distillation: used to separate a mixture of liquids with different boiling points. The fractionating column keeps hotter liquids at the bottom; the most volatile liquid rises to the top and distils off first. Used to separate ethanol from water and to refine crude oil. Chromatography: the mixture is dissolved in a solvent (the mobile phase) and travels up chromatography paper (the stationary phase). Different substances travel different distances because they have different attractions to the paper and different solubilities in the solvent. The Rf value = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent. Each pure substance has a unique Rf value in a given solvent.
writeBullets: Filtration — residue (solid) stays on filter paper; filtrate (liquid) passes through
Evaporation — solvent removed by heating; solid left behind (quick but impure)
Crystallisation — slow cooling gives large pure crystals; better than simple evaporation
Simple distillation — separates solvent from dissolved solid; condenser turns vapour back to liquid
Fractional distillation — separates liquids by different boiling points; used for crude oil and ethanol/water
Chromatography Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent

keyTerms.def: The liquid that passes through the filter paper during filtration
The insoluble solid left behind on the filter paper
Controlled cooling of a concentrated solution to produce pure crystals
Separation of liquids with different boiling points using a fractionating column
Distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent front in chromatography

keyPoints.memTip: For the exam: always link the technique to the PROPERTY being exploited — filtration uses particle size, distillation uses boiling point, chromatography uses solubility.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Rf value calculations are a common 2-mark question. Make sure Mabel can set up the ratio correctly and knows it's always less than 1.

tables.title: Elements vs compounds vs mixtures
rows: Two or more elements chemically bonded
Two or more substances not chemically combined
Each substance keeps its own properties
Cannot be broken down by chemistry
Salt water, air, crude oil, brass

0.question: Which of the following is a compound, not a mixture?
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right! Water is a compound — hydrogen and oxygen are chemically bonded in a fixed 2:1 ratio. You need a chemical reaction to split them apart (electrolysis).
0.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Air, salt water and brass are all mixtures — their components are not chemically bonded and can be separated by physical methods. Water (H₂O) is a compound because hydrogen and oxygen are chemically bonded together.

2.question: You have a mixture of sand and salt dissolved in water. Which pair of techniques would separate the sand and then obtain pure salt crystals?
2.correctFeedback: Perfect technique selection! Filter first to remove the insoluble sand, then crystallise the salt solution (heat to concentrate, cool slowly to form crystals). That gives you pure salt.
2.wrongFeedback: Think about what you need to do at each step. Sand is insoluble — so filtration removes it. The remaining salt solution needs crystallisation (not distillation — that would give you water, not salt crystals).

3.question: In paper chromatography, a substance travels 4.5 cm while the solvent travels 9.0 cm. What is its Rf value?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! Rf = 4.5 ÷ 9.0 = 0.5. The Rf value is always between 0 and 1 — it's a fraction of how far the substance travels compared to the solvent.
3.wrongFeedback: Remember: Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent = 4.5 ÷ 9.0 = 0.5. The Rf value must always be between 0 and 1 — if you got 2.0, you divided the wrong way round.

commonMistakes: Confusing compounds and mixtures: water is a COMPOUND (chemically bonded), salt water is a MIXTURE (just dissolved). The key question is: are the atoms chemically bonded?
Saying distillation separates a solid from a liquid — it separates LIQUIDS by boiling point (or a solvent from a dissolved solid). Use filtration or crystallisation for solids.
Getting Rf value upside down — it's substance distance ÷ SOLVENT distance, not the other way round. Rf is always less than 1.
Thinking that because a compound contains element X, it has the same properties as X — sodium chloride is safe, but sodium metal and chlorine gas are both hazardous. Compounds are totally different.

Saying 'fractional distillation' when they mean 'simple distillation' — fractional distillation is specifically for separating liquids with different boiling points, not for separating a solid dissolved in a liquid.
examTips: When asked to choose a separation technique, always justify it by linking to a PROPERTY: 'filtration is used because the sand is insoluble in water' — that's the mark-winning sentence.
If a question says 'suggest why the Rf values are different for two substances', the answer is that they have different solubilities in the solvent and/or different attractions to the paper.
For naming compounds: check for -ide (two elements) or -ate/-ite (contains oxygen too). This can help you work out the formula from the name and vice versa.
Remember that chromatography can also identify unknown substances — if an unknown substance has the same Rf value as a known substance in the same solvent, they are likely the same compound.
revisionCardBullets: Element — one type of atom only; cannot be broken down chemically
118 elements; symbols have capital first letter (Na, Fe, Cl, O, Au)
Compound — 2+ elements chemically bonded; fixed ratio; different properties from elements
Mixture — not chemically combined; variable composition; keeps own properties
Separated by physical methods: filtration, evaporation, crystallisation, distillation, chromatography
Filtration — insoluble solid from liquid (residue stays, filtrate passes through)
Crystallisation — controlled cooling gives large pure crystals
Simple distillation — solvent from dissolved solid; fractional distillation — liquids by boiling point
Chromatography — separates by solubility and attraction to paper
Rf value = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent (always 0–1)
Compound needs CHEMICAL reaction to separate; mixture needs PHYSICAL method only
